content:
{
  "title": "FactCheck-Prompting: Adaptive Citation Prompts for Reducing LLM Hallucinations",
  "problem": "Large language models often produce plausible-sounding but false statements, particularly when answering open-domain factual queries. Existing prompting methods do not consistently enforce traceable factuality.",
  "motivation": "Hallucinations erode trust in LLMs and limit their adoption in knowledge-intensive applications. Improving factual accuracy at the prompt level, rather than solely through post-processing or model retraining, could enable safer, more reliable deployment of LLMs in dynamic contexts.",
  "method": "We propose FactCheck-Prompting: for each query, the prompt includes a template asking the model to answer and then cite the exact reference or evidence supporting its statements, e.g., 'Answer the question, then write the Wikipedia article title(s) you used.' Compared to standard prompting, this method explicitly conditions the model to ground its output. The core assumption is that explicit citation requests will constrain generation and reduce hallucinations, as LLMs are less likely to fabricate when required to name sources.",
  "experiment_plan": "Use a subset of the Natural Questions and ELI5 datasets, comparing standard prompting to FactCheck-Prompting. Baseline is vanilla generation; metrics include factuality (human or LLM-based fact-checking), citation accuracy (does source exist and match content), and hallucination rate. A/B test prompts on GPT-4 and Llama-2 to evaluate generality."
}
